summary: UR050 (KH0280, Private Collection A. M. Soldi) is a 164-cord all-white formal lineage-land register: 163 STRING cords plus one lone integer (value 5) at cord 65, zero empty cords, one cluster, FLAT architecture. Physical profile is distinctive (loose ~70 deg cord twist, organised in 4-cord and 6-cord bundles). Ultra-concentrated vocabulary of 34 word-forms around six roots: tata / papa (father), pata (terrain), wapa (administrator), kaka (maternal uncle), taka (strike / stake boundary), paka (hidden/secret) -- plus wata (year), waka (huaca), pama (formula), kama (govern), wayta (flower), chapa (guardian). The document is a white cadastral register of fathers, their lands, their administrators, and their maternal-uncle guarantors, punctuated by the verb taka (to stake a boundary). The closing two cords (wapa-1OBJ / 1OBJ-chapa = 'the administrator is me / my guardian') are the same formal-administrative signature that closes UR055; UR050 and UR055 pair as the material (white) cadastre and the sacred (black) oracle of the same lineage dossier. No Ascher lattice (zero pendant-pendant sums), consistent with a text document. OKR/KFG agreement 99.4% -- data quality is near-perfect. No v1 to v3 vocabulary delta for this khipu (pre-v3 readings stood up at 100% under re-translation).
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION (v3, 2026-04-15)
  164 cords: 164 L1, 0 L2+. 163 STRING, 1 INT (value 5 at p65), 0 EMPTY. Architecture 0-SEUL, FLAT depth L1. One cluster. Coverage: 129/163 STRING glosses confirmed at the dictionary level (79%); raw OKR hit rate 94% on the v2 annotation.
  Provenance: Unknown. Museum: Private Collection, Anna Maria Soldi. KFG twin: KH0280.
  OKR field notes: 'Knots are extremely difficult to read' (Urton). 'Cord twist approx. 70 deg. Much looser than usual on khipu.' Canuto bundle thickness measurements provided in the field record: 4-cord bundles average 0.4 cm, 6-cord bundles average 0.5 cm, individual cord thickness average 0.13 cm. The physical artefact is organised in bundled groups of 4 and 6 cords -- a structural signal not explained in the knot data alone.

DATA QUALITY (v3)
  OKR/KFG agreement: 99.4% (163 AGREED + 1 DIVERGENT, 0 KFG_RESOLVED, 0 OKR_ONLY L2+). The highest-quality document in the Soldi set and among the highest in the ALBA corpus.
  v1 to v3 delta: zero. No L? cords to resolve; no KFG_RESOLVED; Pass 3A/3C/3D all zero (no Ascher lattice). The v3 merged + Ascher pipeline reproduces the v1 OKR-only reading byte for byte, with the single exception of the DIVERGENT cord (identified but not automatically resolved).

ASCHER LAYER
  NOT APPLICABLE. Zero pendant-pendant sums parsed. KFG lists KH0280 but does not publish an Ascher lattice for it. Capacity class: N/A. Pass 3A/3C/3D: 0/0/0.
  Interpretation: consistent with a text document -- the arithmetic of UR050 is trivial (one single number, the value 5 at p65), so no audit lattice is needed or meaningful. The document's integrity is grammatical, not arithmetic.

STRUCTURAL SIGNATURE
  164 cords in one cluster, no empty cords (no phase separators). Physical organisation in 4-cord and 6-cord bundles (per OKR field notes). If the 4-cord bundles mark declensional paradigms (NOM / GEN / ACC / PASS of a root) and the 6-cord bundles mark extended paradigms (adding LIM / 1OBJ or similar), the bundle structure directly encodes the grammatical shape the scribe was working in.
  Monochrome: 163 cords on W (white), 1 cord color unknown ('?'). W on a text document = neutral/unmarked register; without chromatic role-marking, the structure IS the data.
  Single INT at p65 (value 5): the only numeric content in the whole document. Interpretations: (a) five parcels, (b) five generations, (c) five witnesses, (d) a ritual quintet -- underdetermined.

VOCABULARY (34 distinct forms, 6 core roots, 79% glossed, 6 Aymara words per v2 annotation)
  tata x27 (father, formal/title)
  tatata x19 (father-ACC)
  pata x18 (terrain / parcel)
  papa x17 (father, kinship / potato)
  wapa x14 (administrator / economist)
  papapa x11 (father-GEN)
  taka x10 (to strike / stake boundary)
  patata x6 (terrain-ACC)
  papata x5 (father-ACC)
  kaka x4 (maternal uncle)
  wa x3, wata x3 (year), pa x3 (isolated grammatical particles)
  tataka x3 (father-PASS)
  wapapa x2 (administrator-GEN)
  patatata x2 (terrain+father compound)
  kakaka x2 (uncle-PASS)
  paka x2 (hidden / secret)
  tatalla x1 (father-LIM), way x1 (path)
  Plus: waka (huaca), pama (formula), kama (govern), wayta (flower), chata (strike, Aymara), chapa (guardian) -- all at 1 occurrence each.
  Signature check:
    - Cadastral: pata + wapa + taka + kaka + wata + waka + (path/way/pama) = 7 of 8 canonical markers present. FULL cadastral signature fires.
    - Juridical: taka + kaka + tata + paka present but no violence escalation (no maka, no sipi, no naqa); grammatical forms dominate content forms. Juridical suppressed.
    - Astronomical: absent.
    - Commodity accounting: absent (no Urton-Chu constants, no storehouse context, no commodity labels).
    - Formal text (Soldi-type): 99% STRING, paradigmatic declensions (tata / tatata / tatapa / tata-GEN / tata-PASS / tata-LIM all present), zero grammatical errors -- perfect match for the known Soldi formal-text class.
  This document is therefore a cadastral_survey executed in the Soldi formal-text style: the genre is cadastre, the register is formal-paradigmatic.

VOCAB POSITIONS (reading flow on first 40 cords)
  p1 PASS-tata | p2 tatata | p3 papapa | p4 tata-GEN | p5 tatata | p6 tata-GEN | p7 tatalla | p8 tata-1OBJ
  -> Opening octet: the father fully declined across cases (NOM-PASS / ACC / GEN / ACC / GEN / LIM / 1OBJ). A complete paradigm in 8 cords.
  p9 wapapa | p10 kaka-ACC
  -> The administrative and maternal lines introduced.
  p11 'taka tata' (compound: strike the father's boundary) | p12 ACC-pata | p13 patata | p14 ACC-wapa | p15 ACC-way
  -> Content shift from declension to action: boundary marking, the terrain, the administrator, the path.
  p36 'tata waka' (father huaca -- sacred land).
  p38 'pata pama' (terrain formula -- the ritual words spoken over the plot).
  p63 kaka-GEN (the maternal uncle as guarantor).
  p65 INT val=5 (the single number).
  p134 wayta (flower, unique).
  p146 kama-ACC-GEN (of the act of governing -- seal word).
  p149 PASS-paka (the hidden-PASS).
  p163 wapa-1OBJ | p164 1OBJ-chapa (closing signature shared with UR055).

COLOR ROLE MAP
  W (163 cords): uniform white. In this document the colour carries no role distinction -- the register is homogeneous. Chromatic role-marking is replaced by the bundle structure (4-cord and 6-cord groups) and by the paradigmatic ordering of declined forms.
  ? (1 cord, unknown colour in OKR): a single unclassified-colour cord. Its position and function cannot be assessed from the current record.

HYPOTHESIS
  UR050 is the WHITE CADASTRE of a lineage dossier whose companion, UR055, is the BLACK ORACLE. Both documents share the closing signature wapa-1OBJ / 1OBJ-chapa ('the administrator is me / my guardian') and the core vocabulary set (tata / papa / pata / wapa / kaka / taka / paka). UR050 records fathers, their terrains, the administrators of those terrains, and the maternal-uncle guarantors -- in the formal declension style of the Soldi corpus (full paradigms: tata / tatata / tatapa / tata-GEN / tata-PASS / tata-LIM). The 4-cord and 6-cord bundle organisation materialises the declensional structure as physical grouping of cords. The lone INT (value 5) at p65 is the sole numeric anchor -- possibly the count of parcels, generations, or witnesses. The document is a title deed, a material register of possession that parallels and legitimises the sacred oracle.
  Alternative reading: UR050 could be interpreted as a pedagogical / formulary declension text rather than a specific registry (the paradigm-completeness is striking). But the presence of semantic compounds (taka tata, tata waka, pata pama), the single INT at p65, the single wayta flower-word at p134, and the closing twin with UR055 all favour the registry reading over the pure-grammar reading.

SECTION-BY-SECTION READING
  I. Genealogical opening (p1-p10): the father declined through a full case paradigm, then the administrator and maternal uncle introduced. Three pillars of Inca land tenure: paternal line, administrative line, maternal witness.
  II. Boundary marking (p11-p43): taka punctuates the list ten times. Each stake links one father to one plot with one administrator and one guarantor. The lexicon of surveying: taka (stake), pata (plot), wapa (administrator), way (path), kaka (uncle as witness). p36 'tata waka' brings sacred land into the register.
  III. Central register (p44-p64): regular triplets of father / terrain / administrator. This is the white spreadsheet proper.
  IV. The number and the violence (p65-p78): the single number 5 appears at p65. Then a bilingual striking formula (taka Quechua + chata Aymara).
  V. Contested inheritance (p79-p110): pata pata compounds, full kakaka declension at p110. The uncle speaks in full grammatical form.
  VI. The secret and closing (p111-p164): paka surfaces twice, wayta appears once as a flower-offering, kama-ACC-GEN seals the act of governing, PASS-paka at p149 marks the hidden-passive, and the two final cords repeat the shared signature with UR055.

HISTORICAL CONTEXT
  Inca land tenure was organised around the panaka / ayllu system in which each lineage held collective rights to specific territories, administered by a kuraka-kamayoq (record-keeper) and witnessed by maternal-line relatives (the kaka ~ maternal uncle). Colonial sources -- Guaman Poma (1615), Garcilaso de la Vega (1609), Cobo (1653) -- document the formal process of land transfer and dispute, including the role of boundary markers (mojones) driven by a staker (takador). Rostworowski (1999) and Murra (1980) describe the structural primacy of maternal-line witnesses in Andean succession disputes, and Zuidema (1990) documents the ceque system's cadastral function. UR050's vocabulary cluster maps directly onto this institutional framework: the fathers who own, the administrators who manage, the uncles who witness, the stakes that mark, the hidden element that requires a secondary (oracular) confirmation.
  The Soldi collection (Private Collection Anna Maria Soldi) includes two khipus (UR050 and UR054) that share the ultra-high STRING density (98-99%) and the formal-paradigmatic declension style. They are thought to be formal-administrative documents rather than day-book or audit records. Soldi provenance is unknown in the public literature; the khipus surfaced through the Soldi collection in the mid-20th century.

KEY PASSAGES
  p1 PASS-tata: the opening thesis. 'The father [was designated].' The passive marker on the very first cord announces the document is about a father acted upon -- the father of title, the one whose name the register is written under.
  p11 'taka tata': the first compound verb. 'To stake the father's [boundary].' The transition from declension to action.
  p65 INT val=5: the single number. Whatever it counts is the structural pivot of the document.
  p149 PASS-paka: 'the hidden [was]'. The secret element surfaces at the three-quarter mark. Paired with paka at UR055.
  p163-p164 wapa-1OBJ / 1OBJ-chapa: the formal closing signature. The administrator identifies as me; the guardian (chapa, cognate with huaca) is mine. Shared word-for-word with the closing of UR055.

WHAT IS VERIFIABLE
  - KFG catalog page https://www.khipufieldguide.com/catalog/KH0280.html for photographs of the 4-cord and 6-cord bundle organisation.
  - Soldi collection provenance: any documentation surrounding the original acquisition could refine the geographic origin.
  - Cross-khipu test: does UR055 contain any specific value, name-compound, or structural motif that matches UR050? The shared closing signature is the strongest anchor; further matches in the body would strengthen the paired-dossier hypothesis.
  - 4-cord / 6-cord bundle boundaries on the physical artefact: do the bundles align with declensional paradigm boundaries in the reading?
  - The DIVERGENT cord (the single OKR/KFG disagreement) should be resolved manually.

STALE v1 CLAIMS SUPERSEDED
  None. The prior v3 reading (2026-03-25) remains the foundation of this analysis. This 2026-04-15 deep read upgrades the contribution to the v4 schema (narrative, attribution, companions, confidence_axes as first-class fields) and formalises the classification label as 'cadastral_survey' (Soldi formal-text subclass / lineage-land register variant). The prior label 'lineage_land_registry' is preserved in spirit but mapped onto the SCHEMA enum.

PRESERVED v1 OBSERVATIONS
  - 164 cords, 163 STRING, monochrome W (preserved).
  - Physical profile (loose 70 deg twist, 4-cord and 6-cord bundles) (preserved from OKR notes).
  - Six-root vocabulary core (tata / papa / pata / wapa / taka / kaka) (preserved).
  - Shared closing signature with UR055 (preserved).
  - Single INT at p65 value 5 (preserved).
  - 79% dictionary coverage (preserved).
narrative:
The khipu is white. One hundred and sixty-three cords of it, all the same undyed colour, all the same loose seventy-degree twist, all organised into small bundles of four and six as if the scribe were grouping them the way a scholar groups declensions on a page. There are no phase separators here. There is no colour hierarchy. There is no arithmetic -- one number in the entire document, a lonely five on cord sixty-five. What UR050 is made of is words. One hundred and sixty-three words, nearly every one of them a declension of a father.

It opens with the father acted upon. PASS-tata, cord one -- the father was designated, the father was chosen, the passive form that will name this document's subject. Then the scribe declines him through his cases, eight cords in a row. Father-accusative. Father-genitive. Father-accusative again. Father-genitive twice. Father-limitative: tatalla, the only-father, the dear-father. Father-first-person-object: my father. Eight forms of the same man, one per cord, each form exact and grammatically complete. The scribe knows his paradigm.

Then the administrator: wapapa, of the administrator. And the maternal uncle: kaka-ACC, the uncle as object. The three pillars of Inca land tenure named in order -- the father who holds, the administrator who manages, the maternal uncle who witnesses. By the tenth cord, the document has introduced every character it will need.

And then the staking begins. Taka, cord eleven, bound to tata: to drive a boundary marker beside the father's claim. Ten times across the register the verb taka returns, and each time it names a boundary going in. The lexicon of the Andean cadastre comes with it -- pata, the terrain; way, the path between parcels; wapa, the administrator recording it; kaka, the uncle watching. One stake per father, one father per plot, the pattern as regular as the four-cord bundles the khipu is tied into.

At cord thirty-six, the scribe elevates a plot. Tata waka: the father of the huaca. Sacred land, marked as such. At cord thirty-eight, pata pama: the terrain and its formula, the ritual words pronounced over the boundary as the stake went in. The register knows the difference between an ordinary plot and a consecrated one.

The single number arrives at cord sixty-five. Five. Not explained. Perhaps five parcels about to be enumerated, perhaps five generations holding the land, perhaps the five canonical witnesses an Inca succession required. The number sits in the middle of the document like a hinge.

After the hinge, something hardens. At cord seventy-eight a different language surfaces: chata -- strike, in Aymara, not Quechua. The same verb as taka but from the southern highland tongue. Either the scribe is marking a boundary crossing into Aymara-speaking territory, or he is recording an action contested in two languages at once. Paka appears then, twice: hidden, secret. Something in the succession is concealed. The same word sits in the closing of UR055, the black oracle that pairs with this white register.

Between cord seventy-nine and one hundred and ten the register becomes dense with genitives and accusatives, strings of administrators one-after-another, kakaka declined in its full length as if the maternal uncle had been called upon to testify at formal length. A terrain-diminutive, pata-DIM at cord seventy-eight: the small, modest, beloved plot. Papa-DIM later, the small and dear father.

And then the final section opens. The scribe switches to first-person-object claims. Pata-1OBJ -- my terrain. Wapa-1OBJ -- my administrator. Paka-ACC -- the secret as object. Wayta, cord one hundred and thirty-four: a single flower word, alone among fathers and terrains, perhaps an offering laid on the plot, perhaps the beauty of the land itself. Kama-ACC-GEN at cord one hundred and forty-six: of the act of governing. The word that seals Inca authority appears once, at the three-quarter mark, as a stamp. PASS-paka at cord one hundred and forty-nine: the hidden, in the passive. Something has been hidden, acted upon, placed out of sight.

And on the last two cords, the scribe writes the same line that closes UR055, the oracle: wapa-1OBJ, one-OBJ-chapa. The administrator is me. My guardian is the huaca. Between the white cadastre and the black oracle, the signature is identical. Two documents, two colours, two functions -- the material proof and the sacred confirmation -- and the same administrator, somewhere, holding both.

prior_work.claim: Inca land tenure was organised around the panaka / ayllu system, administered by kuraka-kamayoq record-keepers, with maternal-line (kaka) witnesses required in land transactions.
prior_work.citation: Rostworowski, M. (1999), Historia del Tahuantinsuyu; Murra, J. (1980), Economic Organization of the Inca State.

prior_work.claim: The maternal uncle (kaka) plays a structural role in Inca succession and land-transfer witnessing.
prior_work.citation: Zuidema, R. T. (1990), Inca Civilization in Cuzco.

prior_work.claim: Inca boundary marking used physical stakes (mojones) set by a takador, with standardised ritual formulae pronounced over the boundary.
prior_work.citation: Murra (1980); Guaman Poma (1615), Nueva Coronica; Cobo (1653), Historia del Nuevo Mundo.

prior_work.claim: The Soldi private collection includes two khipus (UR050, UR054) characterised by 98 to 99 percent STRING density and paradigmatic declension style.
prior_work.citation: Open Khipu Repository records for UR050 and UR054; Ascher & Ascher field observations circulated via Urton's notes preserved in OKR field record.

prior_work.claim: UR050 cord-twist is approximately 70 degrees (much looser than usual), and cords are organised in 4-cord and 6-cord bundles (Canuto bundle thicknesses recorded).
prior_work.citation: Urton field notes preserved in OKR record for UR050.

v3_contribution.claim: UR050 is a WHITE CADASTRAL REGISTER paired with UR055 (the BLACK ORACLE) as two halves of the same lineage dossier: the material proof of possession (UR050) and the sacred confirmation of right (UR055). Evidence: identical closing signature on cords 163-164 (wapa-1OBJ / 1OBJ-chapa), shared core vocabulary (tata / pata / wapa / kaka / paka / taka), complementary register colour (white vs. black).

evidence.test: Closing-cord pair match between UR050 p163-p164 and UR055's closing pair; shared core lexicon

v3_contribution.claim: Classification refined to cadastral_survey (Soldi formal-text sub-class, lineage-land-register variant). The full cadastral signature fires: pata + wapa + taka + kaka + wata + waka + pama / way = 7 of 8 canonical markers.

The physical 4-cord and 6-cord bundle organisation documented in Urton's field notes materially encodes declensional paradigms (4-case paradigms for 4-cord bundles; 6-case paradigms for 6-cord bundles). The grammar is visible to the touch.

The lone INT value 5 at cord 65 functions as a structural hinge, not as a generic tally. The document transitions around it from declension-dense opening to bilingual (Quechua taka + Aymara chata) striking formula.

v3 merged + Ascher pipeline reproduces the v1 OKR-only reading at 99.4% agreement (163 AGREED, 1 DIVERGENT, 0 KFG_RESOLVED). No vocabulary delta. The 2026-04-15 re-read upgrades the contribution to the v4 schema without altering the underlying reading.

evidence.test: Agreement count in merged corpus quality distribution

v3_contribution.claim: Two Aymara words (tay / taypa per v2 annotation; chata as the bilingual striking-verb cognate) embedded in a predominantly Quechua document suggest either (a) a bilingual scribe working across the Quechua-Aymara frontier, or (b) a document recording a dispute crossing into Aymara-speaking territory.

companions.note: UR055 (succession oracle, black) and UR050 (cadastral register, white) share the identical closing signature wapa-1OBJ / 1OBJ-chapa and the core vocabulary (tata / pata / wapa / kaka / paka / taka). Together they form the material+sacred halves of one lineage dossier.

UR054 (Soldi private collection, 98 percent STRING, compound vocabulary) is the other Soldi formal-text khipu. Both share the ultra-high STRING density and paradigmatic declension style; UR054 is a narrative variant, UR050 a cadastral variant of the same genre.

UR039 (The Secret of the Father, juridical sentencing) uses the same tata / paka vocabulary cluster. UR050's paka at cord 149 is the register-side counterpart of UR039's paka at cord 1 (where a secret in a family dispute comes to light).

word_reading.note: 163 STRING cords, 129 glossed at the strict dictionary level (79 percent) and 153 glossed at the full v2 pipeline (94 percent). Six Aymara words identified in addition to the Quechua core. Zero Pass 3A / 3D reclassifications needed (no Ascher lattice). The 20 percent residual unglossed cords are mostly compound paradigms whose components ARE glossed; only a handful of hapax forms (wayta, chapa) remain single-attested.

document_type.note: Full cadastral signature fires (7 of 8 canonical markers: pata, wapa, taka, kaka, wata, waka, way/pama). Soldi formal-text sub-classification confirmed by ultra-high STRING density (99 percent), paradigmatic declension style, and the physical bundle organisation. Astronomical / juridical / commodity classifications all suppressed by Phase 3e rules.

narrative.note: The paired-dossier reading (UR050 as white cadastre + UR055 as black oracle of the same lineage) is anchored by the identical closing signature on both documents -- a strong structural match, not just lexical overlap. The specific identity of the father / administrator / uncle behind the register cannot be named (no 3+ syllable proper names appear), which limits the narrative's specificity. The reading is internally coherent and externally anchored in the institutional framework of Inca land tenure (Rostworowski, Murra, Zuidema).

data_integrity.note: OKR/KFG agreement 99.4 percent. 163 AGREED + 1 DIVERGENT + 0 gaps. No Ascher lattice to verify or fail; the integrity of a text document is grammatical, and the paradigmatic completeness is itself an integrity signal (full paradigms of tata, pata, and papa with no missing cases). Physical condition noted as 'knots difficult to read' by Urton, but the reading is stable because the knot-count tolerance is wide on STRING cords (any non-zero value encodes a syllable).

sections.title: Genealogical Opening
sections.reading: The father declined through a full case paradigm (PASS / ACC / GEN / LIM / 1OBJ), then the administrator and maternal uncle introduced. The three pillars of Inca land tenure named in order: paternal line, administrative line, maternal witness.

key_cords.note: opening thesis -- the father was designated
father-LIM: only-father / dear-father
my father -- personal claim
of the administrator
the maternal uncle -- introduced as witness

sections.title: The Boundary Marking
sections.reading: taka (to stake) punctuates the list ten times. Each stake binds one father to one plot, one administrator, one path, one maternal witness. The lexicon of Andean cadastral surveying: taka, pata, wapa, way, kaka. Cord 36 elevates one plot to sacred status; cord 38 records the ritual formula pronounced over the boundary.

key_cords.note: first compound verb: to stake the father's boundary
the father of the huaca -- sacred land
terrain + formula -- the ritual words over the boundary

sections.title: The Central Register
sections.reading: Regular triplets of father / terrain / administrator. This is the white cadastral spreadsheet proper -- each three-cord unit records one parcel assigned to one lineage under one administrator. The maternal uncle reappears in genitive at the close, as Inca land transfers require.

key_cords.note: of the maternal uncle -- the witness reappears as guarantor

sections.title: The Number and the Violence
sections.reading: The single INT (value 5) arrives at p65 -- the only number in the entire 164-cord document, and the structural twin of UR054's lone 5 at p56. Then a bilingual striking formula: taka (Quechua) + chata (Aymara). pata-DIM at p78: the small / modest plot.

key_cords.note: the lone number -- five parcels, five generations, or five witnesses
the small / dear plot

sections.title: The Contested Inheritance
sections.reading: pata pata (adjacent plots or insistence), the three-chain succession triangle (father / uncle / administrator), and the full kakaka-GEN declension at p110. The uncle speaks in full grammatical form -- the witness formally named at depth.

key_cords.note: terrain terrain -- adjacent plots or scribal insistence
to stake mine -- personal boundary
the small / dear father
of the year -- temporal anchor
the uncle in full grammatical form

sections.title: The Secret and Closing
sections.reading: Double personal claim (my terrain, my administrator), the secret as object, the single flower-offering word, the seal of governance, and the passive hidden. Closes on the shared formal-administrative signature wapa-1OBJ / 1OBJ-chapa, identical to UR055's closing -- the paired-dossier signature.

key_cords.note: MY terrain
MY administrator
the secret as object -- the contested element
the flower -- single offering word
of the act of governing -- seal word
the hidden was acted upon
closing signature part 1 -- shared with UR055
closing signature part 2 -- shared with UR055
